# Human Oversight Spectrum

## The Physics

Human involvement in AI-assisted work is not binary (human does it vs. AI does it).
It is a spectrum with four distinct levels, each appropriate for different risk
profiles and trust levels:

| Level | Name | Human Role | When to Use |
|-------|------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | Human Decides | Actor | High-stakes, irreversible, novel |
| 2 | Human Approves | Gatekeeper | Significant impact, needs judgment |
| 3 | Human Audits | Auditor | Routine but consequential |
| 4 | Agent-Governed | Escalation endpoint | Well-defined, reversible, proven |

**Universal principle**: No work exists without a review trail. Even at Level 4,
reviewing agents check work, and humans audit the review process periodically.
The oversight never disappears — it shifts from direct to supervisory.

## Why It Matters

- **Regulatory compliance**: EU AI Act, SOX 302, and industry frameworks require defined oversight.
- **Risk calibration**: Oversight proportional to risk, not uniform across all tasks.
- **Efficiency**: Over-supervising routine tasks wastes human attention.
- **Safety**: Under-supervising novel tasks creates unacceptable risk.

## Academic/Industry Backing

- EU AI Act Article 14: human oversight requirements.
- NIST AI RMF: risk-proportional governance.
- SAE Levels for autonomous vehicles: graduated human involvement.
- Anthropic agent safety framework: monitoring proportional to capability.

## Connected Objects

- JOS-C16 (Trust Spectrum) — oversight level follows trust level
- JOS-P01 (Trust & Graduated Autonomy) — the principle governing the spectrum
- JOS-P04 (Mandatory Escalation) — the mechanism when oversight detects issues
